title: Partition Array for Maximum XOR and AND
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals for LeetCode 3630. The core reduction enumerates B, then maximizes the A/C split with a masked XOR linear basis.

variants.languageReason: C++ gives native integer loops, compact subset tables, fixed stack basis storage, and direct access to bit intrinsics for the O(2^n * n * w) hot path.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It best balances fast subset enumeration, compact vectors, fixed arrays, and LeetCode's C++ method signature.
Considered for raw arrays, but the judge signature is C++ and C-style code would not reduce the algorithmic work.
Native and safe, but checked indexing and LeetCode boilerplate add overhead without improving this dense bitmask loop.
Compiled and clear, but bounds checks and runtime overhead are less favorable for repeated basis rebuilds.
Primitive arrays can work, but VM and bounds-check overhead make them weaker for top native runtime.
The constraints are small but still require hundreds of thousands of basis rebuilds, so interpreter/VM overhead is not ideal.

variants.approachJustification: Precompute subset XOR and AND values, enumerate every B mask, and solve the remaining A/C split through a masked XOR basis.

variants.languageReason: C++ static buffers, local sorting, upper-bound pruning, and direct bit intrinsics reduce allocation and per-mask basis work for the runtime-focused version.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Static arrays, sorted local values, exact mask upper bounds, early full-rank detection, and intrinsic bit operations target the best wall-clock constants.
Could implement similar buffers, but it does not match the platform's class method interface as naturally.
Can be optimized, but safe indexing and more verbose static-buffer management are unlikely to beat tuned C++ here.
Bounds checks and less direct static storage make it less compelling for the speed extreme.
Managed static primitive arrays are possible, but VM overhead and checks are not ideal for this tight loop.
Not competitive for the runtime objective because the hot path is millions of small integer reductions.

variants.approachJustification: Use the same exact basis reduction, but skip masks whose upper bound cannot improve the answer and stop basis construction when all free bits are spanned.

variants.languageReason: C++ minimizes auxiliary storage to scalar state and a fixed 30-entry stack basis while preserving native bit operations.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It keeps the memory footprint to a tiny fixed basis under the required C++ LeetCode signature.
Could be similarly lean, but the LeetCode interface is already C++ and no memory is saved by dropping C++ syntax.
Memory-safe and compact, but the C++ stack-array implementation is simpler and equally lean.
Runtime and slice overhead are larger than a fixed C++ stack array for this objective.
Heap-backed primitive arrays and VM overhead are not as memory-frugal as C++ stack state.
Object-heavy integer storage is unsuitable for the least-memory target.

variants.approachJustification: Recompute AND(B) and remaining XOR for each mask, avoiding subset tables while reusing the same masked basis formula.

reasoningSummary: Enumerating the AND subset B reduces the rest of the partition to maximizing x + (s xor x). The identity x + (s xor x) = s + 2 * (x & ~s) turns that into a maximum subset XOR over masked remaining values, solved by a greedy linear basis.
